# Forensic Epidemiology: Bridging Public Health and Legal Investigations to Address Modern Challenges

First published: 06 March 2025

**Editor:** Claude Roux

**Funding:** The authors received no specific funding for this work.

## ABSTRACT

Forensic epidemiology (FE) is a multi-disciplinary field that applies epidemiologic principles and methods to collate and analyze trends and associations among medical and scientific examinations of adverse health outcomes with criminal or other legal implications. FE has been applied to a range of issues including bioterrorism, mass casualty events, and occupational hazards to inform public health and legal responses. This review explores the historical evolution, primary methodologies, and most common contemporary applications of FE, emphasizing the contribution of forensic medicine and science to public health and safety. Key methods such as descriptive and analytical epidemiology, biostatistical analyses, and field investigations are discussed, highlighting their application in bioterrorism responses, toxicological investigations, death inquiries, and public health policy development. The review underscores the strengths of FE, including its objective, evidence-based framework and multi-disciplinary collaboration, while addressing challenges like data limitations, ethical considerations, and the complexity of demonstrating causation in legal contexts. By applying epidemiologic principles and methods to analyze the findings from forensic investigations, FE not only supports the resolution of individual health-related legal disputes but also has applications at the population level for public health interventions. These findings demonstrate the growing importance of FE as a vital tool in addressing the complexities of modern forensic and public health challenges.

## Graphical Abstract

Image generated using Canva's Text-to-Image feature, www.canva.com/ai-image-generator [4 February 2025].

## 1 Introduction

In 2001, anthrax-laced letters were mailed to media outlets and U.S. Senators, resulting in five deaths and multiple cases of anthrax infection (Atlas [2002](#wfs270003-bib-0003)). The Federal Bureau of Investigation (FBI) launched a collaborative investigation with epidemiologists and other experts to trace the source of the anthrax and identify the perpetrator (Goodman et al. [2003](#wfs270003-bib-0035)). By analyzing population-level victim data, assessing the distribution of contaminated letters, and tracking exposure patterns, epidemiologists linked the anthrax to Dr. Bruce Ivins, a microbiologist at the U.S. Army Medical Research Institute of Infectious Diseases (USAMRIID) at Fort Detrick, Maryland, who died in 2008 from suicide before being officially charged (Nasaw [2008](#wfs270003-bib-0058)). The case demonstrated how traditional epidemiological methods could be applied in a forensic context to solve a complex bioterrorism crime, effectively bridging the disciplines of public health and forensic science.

Forensic epidemiology (FE) applies public health scientific principles and methods to identify and analyze contributory and causative factors using information generated during a medicolegal investigation (Meilia, Zeegers, Herkutanto, and Zeegers [2020](#wfs270003-bib-0052); Meilia, Zeegers, Herkutanto, and Freeman [2020](#wfs270003-bib-0053)). By partnering public health officers with law enforcement, FE has emerged as a critical tool in attributing adverse health outcomes to one or a combination of exposures to support both criminal and civil legal proceedings (Meilia, Zeegers, Herkutanto, and Zeegers [2020](#wfs270003-bib-0052); Meilia, Zeegers, Herkutanto, and Freeman [2020](#wfs270003-bib-0053); Wecht and Koehler [2003](#wfs270003-bib-0074)). By examining trends and patterns among fatal and nonfatal health outcomes (both natural and unnatural) at a population level, contributory and causative risk factors that may be modifiable through intervention can be identified and mitigated (Wecht and Koehler [2003](#wfs270003-bib-0074)). Ultimately, the objective of FE is to provide evidence-based population data that can be used for the attribution of responsibility in forensic investigations and for recommendations of preventive measures to enhance public health and safety.

## 2 The Forensic Epidemiology Evolution

Epidemiology as we know it today originated in studies of mortality derived from the “Bills of Mortality”—published records of births and deaths by location and cause—in the London metropolis in the early 16th century (Loff and Cordner [2013](#wfs270003-bib-0049)). Collected by parish clerks and printed regularly, the public examined the bills in detail with critical attention to the plague's whereabouts. These vital records were meant for legal purposes until John Graunt (1620–1674) collated them into a series of observations in 1662, making him the founder of demography and epidemiology (Connor [2024](#wfs270003-bib-0018)). In his work, among other contributions, Graunt replaced assumptions with reasoned population estimates and produced the first accurate data on male-to-female ratios. He tabulated health data distinguishing acute, often epidemic diseases from chronic, typically endemic illnesses. Graunt also quantified high infant mortality and calculated a case fatality rate during a fever epidemic, becoming the first to note “excess deaths” in epidemics. His sole publication provided a model for analyzing demographic and health data, introducing statistical association, inference, and population sampling (Sutherland [1963](#wfs270003-bib-0068)).

While individual deaths often seemed random, Graunt observed that causes of death within a population followed predictable patterns. This new concept influenced many pioneers who contributed significantly to the field of epidemiology (El-Gilany [2022](#wfs270003-bib-0025)). But it was not until 1965 that Sir Austin Bradford Hill (1897–1991), the greatest epidemiologist and medical statistician of the 20th century, developed nine “viewpoints” to attribute causation for an observed association in a population: Analogy, biological gradient, coherence, consistency, experiment, plausibility, specificity, strength of association, and temporality (Fedak et al. [2015](#wfs270003-bib-0027)). Often referred to as the “Bradford Hill (BH) Criteria,” it remains the most well known and commonly used approach for inferring causality in epidemiological studies. The application of such epidemiological-based methods within the context of civil or criminal investigations has been the driving force behind the conceptualization and definition of FE.

Investigative responses to public health events possibly involving criminal activity appear to have started at least in the mid-1970s. In 2023, Goodman et al. reviewed 12 cases in the U.S. involving joint investigations by epidemiologists and law enforcement (e.g., CDC—Centers for Disease Control and FBI) between 1975 and 2003 (Goodman et al. [2003](#wfs270003-bib-0035)). These cases included incidents such as Salmonella outbreaks, cardiopulmonary arrests in a hospital, and anthrax exposure, affecting large populations across various settings. The investigations examined events potentially linked to criminal behavior, such as bioterrorism, murder, and assault, and investigators reported the need for enhanced laboratory and public health capabilities to explore potential cause-and-effect relationships in order to better understand disease onset and progression. Since its introduction in the 1990s, the field of FE has continued to evolve, from describing the role of epidemiologists in legal proceedings to addressing public health threats related to forensic investigations (Table [1](#wfs270003-tbl-0001 "Link to table")).

TABLE 1.
Contemporary timeline of forensic epidemiology.

| Year | Definition(s) |
| --- | --- |
| 1990s | Epidemiologists as expert witnesses in civil trials to provide testimony on study methodology (including design, duration, and sample size), interpretation of results, and identified limitations (Loue [1999](#wfs270003-bib-0050)). |
| 1999 | Use of epidemiological data in the processes and the involvement of epidemiologists in judicial proceedings (Loue [1999](#wfs270003-bib-0050)).  Application of epidemiologic tools to help distinguish between natural and man-made epidemics (Koehler and Brown [2010](#wfs270003-bib-0044)). |
| 2001 | Systematic approach to the investigation of an act of bioterrorism, as well as other public health incidents (Koehler and Brown [2010](#wfs270003-bib-0044)). |
| 2003 | Use of epidemiological methods as part of an ongoing investigation of a health problem to determine whether suspicions or evidence of possible intentional acts or criminal behaviors are contributing factors to the health problem (Goodman et al. [2003](#wfs270003-bib-0035)).  Use of epidemiological and other public health methods in conjunction with or as an adjunct to an ongoing criminal investigation (Goodman et al. [2003](#wfs270003-bib-0035)).  Application of epidemiological principles and methods to forensic issues (Wecht and Koehler [2003](#wfs270003-bib-0074)). |
| 2008 | Application of epidemiological concepts and data to forensic issues is the practice of Forensic Epidemiology (Koehler and Freeman [2014](#wfs270003-bib-0045)). |
| 2013 | Use of FE as a method of evaluating causation in groups and individuals based on the application of the Hill criteria, with conclusions given in terms of relative or comparative risk, or as a Probability of Causation (Koehler and Freeman [2014](#wfs270003-bib-0045)). |
| 2015 | A systematic approach to the assessment of general and specific (individual) causation, with the results suitable for presentation in a court of law (Freeman and Zeegers [2015](#wfs270003-bib-0032)). |
| 2020 | A systematic approach to incorporating population-based methods, principles, and data in the process of medicolegal causal evaluation (Meilia, Zeegers, Herkutanto, and Zeegers [2020](#wfs270003-bib-0052); Meilia, Zeegers, Herkutanto, and Freeman [2020](#wfs270003-bib-0053)).  The discipline that is concerned with the use of epidemiology within the practices of forensic medicine (Meilia, Zeegers, Herkutanto, and Zeegers [2020](#wfs270003-bib-0052); Meilia, Zeegers, Herkutanto, and Freeman [2020](#wfs270003-bib-0053)). |

In 1999, the former first deputy chief of the USSR bioweapon program, Dr. Ken Alibek, first used the term FE in association with government investigations of bioterrorism (Loue [1999](#wfs270003-bib-0050)). The 2001 U.S. anthrax attacks underscored the need for joint investigations between law enforcement and public health officials to address such health threats with potential criminal intent (Goodman et al. [2003](#wfs270003-bib-0035)). Consequently, joint CDC –FBI training and resources were developed in 2002 to strengthen collaborations between the two fields, allowing for systematic investigation of incidents with criminal and public health implications. The approach supported both bioterrorism responses and court cases where epidemiological evidence was crucial for causation (Koehler and Freeman [2014](#wfs270003-bib-0045)). By 2003, the increasing impact of these combined efforts on the judicial system as well as public health led to the emergence of FE as a new field of applied science (Wecht and Koehler [2003](#wfs270003-bib-0074)).

Over the next decade, the scope of FE significantly broadened to address a variety of medicolegal issues frequently encountered in both criminal and civil court cases (Koehler and Freeman [2014](#wfs270003-bib-0045)). More specifically, FE bridged clinical findings with epidemiologic methods to assess causation in legal contexts (Freeman and Zeegers [2015](#wfs270003-bib-0032)).

The utility of FE in judicial courts worldwide has increased in recent years, with many legal matters using epidemiologic evidence to assess causation between exposure and adverse health outcomes, e.g., an injury or disease (Freeman et al. [2008](#wfs270003-bib-0030)). Initially focused on general causation (population level), this evidence is now also recognized for specific causation (individual cases). Understanding causation requires risk comparisons rather than direct observation; therefore, the primary goal of an FE analysis is to provide a structured, dependable, and clear approach to addressing relevant legal questions with probabilistic evidence, making it suitable for presentation to a judge or jury in civil or criminal cases.

## 3 Epidemiological Principles and Methods

Epidemiology is the fundamental science of public health and is defined as the study of the distribution and determinants of health conditions (e.g., disease, injury, etc.) in specified populations for the purposes of prevention and control (Bonita et al. [2006](#wfs270003-bib-0010)). As a subfield, FE applies epidemiologic principles and methods to the collation, analysis, and interpretation of medical and scientific findings from adverse health outcomes to identify the presence and association of risk factors to support legal responses. The general procedure for applying an epidemiological approach comprises the following stages: Formulating the aims/questions or hypotheses; selecting the appropriate study design; identifying the sources and systematic collection of relevant data; and selecting the appropriate technique for performing biostatistical analysis.

### 3.1 Formulating the Aims/Questions/Hypotheses

Formulating the aims/questions and/or hypotheses is a crucial early step in applying an epidemiological approach. A model often used in public health to guide the formulation of aims/questions and/or hypotheses is PI/ECO (Population/Patients or Problem; Intervention or Exposure; Comparison or Context; and Outcome) (Roever [2018](#wfs270003-bib-0062)). Depending on the nature of the public health problem, the elements of the model may vary, particularly whether an intervention or an exposure is being examined. An exemplar template is shown in Table [2](#wfs270003-tbl-0002 "Link to table") that defines each of the PI/ECO elements. This approach not only guides the development of the eligibility criteria (i.e., inclusions and exclusions for each element) but also informs the selection of the most appropriate study design.

TABLE 2.
PI/ECOS model template for establishing eligibility criteria.

|  |  |  |  |  |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
|  | Term | Definition | Included | Excluded |
| P | Population | Characteristics of the population or patients of interest (e.g., age, sex, socioeconomic status). |  |  |
| Patients |  |  |
| Problem | Condition, disease, or health issue of interest. |  |  |
| I | Intervention | Treatment or strategy of interest being tested. |  |  |
| E | Exposure | Factor to which the population is exposed. |  |  |
| C | Comparison | Main alternative to compare with the intervention or exposure (e.g., no intervention or exposure).  Note comparison is not always present. |  |  |
| Context | Environment or conditions under which the exposure and effect occur. |  |  |
| O | Outcome | Target results being measured.  Note that there may be more than one. |  |  |

### 3.2 Selecting the Appropriate Study Design

Epidemiological methods comprise a range of study designs, which are broadly classified as descriptive or analytical. Study designs are arranged in a hierarchy based on the strength and rigor of the evidence produced, with descriptive case reports at the lowest end of the evidence hierarchy and randomized controlled trials at the highest end (Figure [1](#wfs270003-fig-0001)). When selecting an appropriate study design, key considerations include the research question (e.g., causal vs. descriptive), the timing of exposure and outcome (e.g., prospective vs. cross-sectional), the availability and characteristics of the study population, and the potential for bias and confounding. Cohort studies are ideal for studying causality over time, case–control studies are useful for rare outcomes, and cross-sectional studies may offer insights into prevalence. Ethical constraints and statistical power also play a crucial role in choosing the appropriate design.

#### 3.2.1 Descriptive Epidemiological Study Designs

Descriptive epidemiological study designs are applied in FE to analyze health-related events in specific populations to report characteristics of people (who), places (where), and time (when) (Fox et al. [2022](#wfs270003-bib-0028)). Descriptive designs examine (i) who is affected, considering factors like age, sex, ethnicity, occupation, socioeconomic status, lifestyle, and other demographics, which help identify vulnerable or targeted groups; (ii) where events occur, analyzing geographical distributions to uncover clusters or hotspots that might point to environmental, occupational, or societal influences; and (iii) when events take place, identifying temporal patterns such as seasonal trends, sudden spikes, or long-term changes that can provide insight into the timing and potential triggers of events. This approach is a critical first step in the process to then identify contributing or causal factors.

Forensic epidemiologists gather data from descriptive studies to generate information-based evidence valuable for legal investigations and public health interventions. Through meticulous collation and analysis of data, descriptive epidemiology can reveal patterns in adverse health outcomes related to intentionally inflicted or unintentional causes, such as poisoning outbreaks or infectious disease clusters in confined populations (Dramowski et al. [2017](#wfs270003-bib-0021)). For instance, a study in 2020 showed that a sudden increase in opioid overdoses within a specific region potentially arose from the introduction of a potent synthetic opioid, requiring further forensic investigation (Jones et al. [2020](#wfs270003-bib-0041); Meyer et al. [2023](#wfs270003-bib-0056)).

Descriptive studies also provide the data required for hypothesis generation, allowing the identification of risk factors associated with specific events to be tested. In forensic contexts, these hypotheses might help identify the source of contamination, the route of exposure, or the timing of an intentional act (Wecht and Koehler [2003](#wfs270003-bib-0074)). Descriptive epidemiology is also invaluable for surveillance and early detection, enabling the monitoring of unusual morbidity and mortality patterns that could signal events like bioterrorism, workplace hazards, or mass poisonings (Fox et al. [2022](#wfs270003-bib-0028); Wenger et al. [2008](#wfs270003-bib-0075)). Understanding baseline frequencies of morbidity and mortality in various settings (e.g., hospital admissions, emergency departments, ambulance attendances, prescription medication utilization, births, and deaths) can improve the detection of sudden or unexpected changes in health outcomes, which may indicate unintentional or deliberate exposures to a given risk (Koehler and Freeman [2014](#wfs270003-bib-0045)). Additionally, the detailed descriptions it provides are crucial for legal and policy actions, supporting claims about causation, negligence, or public health risks.

#### 3.2.2 Analytic Epidemiological Study Designs

Analytical epidemiology is essential in FE because it seeks to uncover causal relationships between exposures, such as toxins, drugs, or pathogens, and outcomes like illness, injury, or death. For example, in cases of suspected poisoning, analytical studies can establish a link between exposure to a specific chemical and the resulting health effects in a population group. This method uses group comparisons (e.g., case–control and cohort studies) and statistical analyses (e.g., hypothesis testing) to determine the how and why behind health events by identifying and analyzing risk factors in specified populations, which is essential in forensic contexts where establishing contribution and causality can provide critical evidence. Case–control studies compare individuals with a specific health condition (cases) to those without it (controls), such as comparing individuals who overdosed on a substance to those who used the substance in question but survived. The aim of this case–control study is to determine a lethal dose and identify potential co-factors contributing to death. Cohort studies, on the other hand, track exposed and unexposed groups over time to assess outcome development, such as monitoring factory workers exposed to a chemical versus nonexposed workers to evaluate long-term health risks. These approaches help pinpoint specific exposures or conditions that contribute to adverse health outcomes.

Analytical epidemiology also plays a critical role in testing hypotheses. It confirms or refutes ideas generated through descriptive methods by employing statistical analyses, described below, to evaluate the strength and significance of associations. For example, in a neighborhood cluster of cancer cases, an analytical study might reveal a statistically significant link to industrial pollution. Additionally, analytical epidemiology helps establish temporal and biological plausibility, assessing whether the timing and biological mechanisms of an exposure can logically explain observed outcomes. For instance, it can determine if exposure to carbon monoxide occurred before a person's death and whether the levels were sufficient to cause observed symptoms.

Moreover, analytical epidemiology quantifies risk by calculating measures like attributable risk, which determines how much of an outcome can be attributed to a specific exposure. For example, it might identify the proportion of drug overdoses caused by fentanyl-laced substances in a region. The causal relationships and risk assessments derived from analytical epidemiology are often critical in strengthening legal evidence. This is particularly valuable in cases involving negligence, occupational hazards, or intentional harm. For example, in an outbreak of Legionnaires' disease, descriptive epidemiology might identify a cluster of cases linked to a specific hotel, while analytical epidemiology could use a case–control study to confirm that exposure to the hotel's cooling system was the source. By applying rigorous analytical methods, FE identifies and quantifies causal links, offering actionable insights and robust evidence for public health interventions, criminal investigations, and legal adjudication.

### 3.3 Identify Sources and Systematic Collection of Data

Where FE identifies patterns or signals particular risks involving causes or contributors to morbidity or mortality, further research may be warranted to address the issue at a population level. Common data sources include public health records (e.g., hospital reports, disease registries), mortality and morbidity statistics, and surveillance systems that track outbreaks or health trends. In criminal cases, forensic autopsies, toxicological reports, and police or legal documents may be used to investigate deaths or injuries linked to environmental or occupational exposures. Wastewater analysis has also become an emerging source for tracking community-wide health trends, such as infectious diseases or drug use patterns. The selection of the right data source is critical to the study's validity and relevance; for instance, using hospital records might be appropriate for studying a specific disease outbreak, while autopsy reports may be more appropriate to investigate external cause injuries. The integrity and reliability of the data source directly impact the quality of the epidemiological analysis and the conclusions that can be drawn.

Data integration or data linkage in FE involves synthesizing diverse datasets to identify patterns, establish causality, and support investigations. Commonly integrated data sources include Medical Examiner and Coroner (MEC) data, public health surveillance systems, environmental monitoring records, law enforcement reports (e.g., witness testimonies), and hospital or laboratory data. MEC data, such as details from autopsies or toxicology reports, often provide granular insights into causes of death, which can be combined with public health records to assess broader trends or identify clusters of disease or injury. National databases containing medicolegal reports (autopsy, toxicology, police, coroner) for reportable cases have been established in some jurisdictions, such as the National Coronial Information System for deaths reported to a Coroner in Australia and New Zealand (Baker et al. [2022](#wfs270003-bib-0006)). Such resources provide an opportunity to identify trends and patterns in death investigations that may inform future public health and policy, coronial inquests, and criminal evidence (Loff and Cordner [2013](#wfs270003-bib-0049)). Similarly, in the United States, there are local, country, and state criminal justice systems that provide access to court and correctional records for research; however, this is known to be expensive and complex (Williams et al. [2023](#wfs270003-bib-0076)).

SUMMARY:

FULL CONTENT:
![PCOM Logo - Home](/_resources/images/logos-and-wordmarks/2024/pcom-dark-bg.png)

# Helping the Living by Learning From the Dead  Forensic Investigation Impacts Public Policy to Save Lives

In 1993, [Dr. Gregory McDonald](/academics/faculty/gregorymcd.html) had one of the most horrific experiences of his career—helping investigate more than
110 heat-related deaths over a span of 10 days—the result of a July heatwave that
had gripped Philadelphia.

![Dr. Gregory McDonald](/_resources/images/faculty/mcdonald-gregory.jpg)

McDonald, who currently serves as director of the [MS in Forensic Medicine program](/academics/programs-and-degrees/forensic-medicine/index.html), was working at the Philadelphia Medical Examiner's Office (MEO) at the time. The
deaths were well in excess of the typical number of homicides, suicides and other
fatalities that would be expected during a comparable time period. He likened the
death toll to a mass casualty situation.

“You can imagine that a plane crash in which 110 people died would certainly get the
attention of people,” he said.

For McDonald and his fellow investigators at the Philadelphia MEO, the particularly
attention-grabbing aspect was that many of the deaths may have been preventable.

## Identifying a Public Health Issue

When assessing the impact of excessive heat or a heatwave, the number of deaths attributable
to hyperthermia is a key indicator. An unusual element of the heatwave of 1993 was
that most of the heat-related deaths did not meet the strict criteria of clinical
hyperthermia. Hyperthermia, McDonald explained, occurs when the body’s temperature
exceeds 104 degrees Fahrenheit. It can encompass a range of symptoms including muscle
cramps, fatigue, dizziness, mental confusion, organ failure and more.

“There’s a spectrum of how these progress,” he said.

When a person is observed with symptoms of heat-related illnesses, medical intervention
may prevent adverse outcomes. For individuals living alone, help may not be readily
available.

According to McDonald, the elderly are particularly at risk.

“They lack some of the thermoregulatory factors that can tell them when they're getting
too hot,” he said. “By the time they realize it, they’re already compromised.”

Many of the heatwave victims were found several days after they died and had other
commonalities—advanced age, preexisting medical conditions and lack of access to a
adequately cooled environment. Several of the victims lived in row homes.

“I grew up in a row home in Philadelphia and literally it's like an oven,” McDonald
said.

Inside temperatures can get very high due to the black tar roofs and lack of cross
ventilation. Oftentimes, there is little or no shade. In more dangerous parts of the
city, residents may keep the windows closed in an attempt to prevent criminal activity.
For the elderly—especially those on fixed incomes—this can be very dangerous.

“Maybe they can't afford air conditioning,” he said. “Even fans, they help move some
air around but sometimes it just makes a convection oven. So then you have people,
they can't get out. They can't hydrate. they start to lose electrolytes, especially
if they're shut-ins or there's not someone checking on them.”

McDonald and other investigators from the Philadelphia MEO made note of numerous other
factors that could have contributed to the deaths including medication use, obesity
and alcohol consumption.

“All those things together can be very difficult to deal with, especially for a compromised
population,” he said. “But again, most of those did not meet these strict criteria
of hyperthermia.”

## Unsung Heroes

From a public health perspective, not being able to attribute the deaths to hyperthermia
was a problem.

“In forensics, when you list an environmental condition, like cold or heat, that takes
it out of the realm of a natural death and puts it into the category of an unnatural
death,” he said. That information is added to the death certificate and can give public
health officials hard data they can use to enact preventative measures.

The forensic investigators at the Philadelphia MEO realized they needed to categorize
the heat-related deaths in a way that better highlighted the risk posed by extreme
heat. By studying the excess deaths during the heatwave, they were able to determine
demographic characteristics and validate the criteria they used to classify a death
as heat-related. The findings, published in [The American Journal of Forensic Medicine and Pathology](https://journals.lww.com/amjforensicmedicine/fulltext/1996/06000/heat_related_deaths_in_philadelphia_1993.4.aspx), listed the criteria for classifying a death as heat-related as including “a frail,
elderly decedent with or without known preexisting disease or a younger decedent with
evidence of preexisting acute or chronic illness and substantial environmental or
circumstantial evidence of heat as a contributing factor to death (i.e., the decedent
is found in a hot environment without cooling devices, and with windows closed).”

As a result of the work done by McDonald and other death investigators at the Philadelphia
MEO, public health measures were put in place to help prevent future heat-related
deaths. These measures included efforts to increase public awareness about the dangers
of excessive heat and providing susceptible populations with access to shelters or
other air-conditioned locations. In the mid-90s, McDonald was part of a task force
that used data collected on death certificates to identify populations to proactively
reach out to in the event of a heatwave.

This, McDonald explained, is just one example of the important role medical examiners
and coroners play in improving public health.

“We’re the unsung heroes,” he said. “People have a perception of us that we only deal
with dead people, that we have no impact on the living and I do think that’s a mistake.
We’re not only a voice for the dead, we can help the living as well.”

enriched_points.expanded_content: Forensic medicine aids in identifying sources and transmission pathways of outbreaks, employing epidemiological methods to analyze health data and implement control measures effectively.

enriched_points.point_title: Role in Identifying Public Health Threats
enriched_points.expanded_content: Forensic medicine detects emerging health threats, linking forensic findings with public health data to inform intervention strategies and mitigate risks to community health.
Forensic professionals collaborate with health agencies, sharing data and expertise to enhance surveillance, response strategies, and public health policies for better community protection.
